coredata.dc:title: Microwave prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid determination of ascorbic acid through a turn off–on strategy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed Abdel Hamid, Mohamed
Elagamy, Samar H.
Gamal, Aya
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: A simple and eco-friendly microwave method was applied for the preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) and used for the determination of ascorbic acid (ASC) in pharmaceutical dosage forms. The prepared NS-CQDs had bright blue fluorescence at a maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm, after excitation with 350 nm, with a quantum yield of 62.5 %. The developed NS-CQDs were prepared from citric acid and l-cysteine in one minute. The native fluorescence of NS-CQDs was quenched by ferric ions due to the formation of non-fluorescent CQDs/ Fe3+ complex. The quenched fluorescence could be restored by the addition of ASC due to the reducing properties of ASC which converts Fe3+ to Fe2+. The method was found linear over the concentration range of 2.0–100 μg/mL, with a limit of detection was 0.6 μg/mL and a coefficient of determination of 0.9965. The proposed method was cross-validated and statistically compared with a reported HPLC method. The results indicated that the developed method was greener, according to the analytical eco-scale and the green analytical procedure index (GAPI). The prepared NS-CQDs were used for spectrofluorometric determination of ASC in pharmaceutical dosage forms, with percentage recoveries ranging between 98 and 102 %, and relative standard deviations less than 2 %. The method was easy, rapid, reliable, and sensitive and did not require expensive reagents or sophisticated equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stock and working solutions for calibration
Selection of the quenching metal cation
Green assessment of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the procedures for the spectrofluorometric determination of ascorbic acid using NS-CQDs.
. A) the tem image of the prepared ns-cqds under 50 nm magnification power b) The TEM image of the prepared NS-CQDS under 100 nm magnification power.
FTIR spectrum showing the characteristic peaks of the prepared NS-CQDS.
Overlay between Excitation (solid lines) and emission spectra (dashed lines) of CQDs (a,a’), CQDS/Fe3+/ASC (b,b’), and CQDS/Fe3+ (c,c’).
Emission fluorescence spectra obtained after adding different ASC concentrations (2,5,10,…,100 μg/mL) to the semi quenched N,S CQDs by ferric ions.
The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) of the proposed method (a) and the reference method (b).
Regression parameters of the developed method for the determination of ascorbic acid.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N.B.: r: correlation coefficient, a: intercept, b: slope, Sy/x: residual standard deviation of the regression line, Sa: standard error of intercept, Sb: standard error of slope, DL: detection limit (calculated), QL: quantitation limit (calculated).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of accuracy, intraday and interday precision of the developed method for the determination of ascorbic acid.
Assessment the greenness of the developed method according to the analytical Eco-scale.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PPs: penalty points. Assessment was done per sample. If the score is >50, it represents acceptable green analysis, if the score is < 50, it represents inadequate green analysis, and if the score is >75, it represents excellent green analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Statistical analysis of the proposed method and reference HPLC method for the determination of ASC in C-VIT oral drops.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aC-VIT® eye drops labeled to contain 160 mg of ASC and 60 mg of ASC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean percentage recovery of three different sample solutions’ % RSD; percentage relative standard deviation.
The theoretical values of f and t test at p =.05.

head.ce:title: Microwave prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid determination of ascorbic acid through a turn off–on strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and eco-friendly microwave method was applied for the preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) and used for the determination of ascorbic acid (ASC) in pharmaceutical dosage forms. The prepared NS-CQDs had bright blue fluorescence at a maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm, after excitation with 350 nm, with a quantum yield of 62.5 %. The developed NS-CQDs were prepared from citric acid and l-cysteine in one minute. The native fluorescence of NS-CQDs was quenched by ferric ions due to the formation of non-fluorescent CQDs/ Fe3+ complex. The quenched fluorescence could be restored by the addition of ASC due to the reducing properties of ASC which converts Fe3+ to Fe2+. The method was found linear over the concentration range of 2.0–100 μg/mL, with a limit of detection was 0.6 μg/mL and a coefficient of determination of 0.9965. The proposed method was cross-validated and statistically compared with a reported HPLC method. The results indicated that the developed method was greener, according to the analytical eco-scale and the green analytical procedure index (GAPI). The prepared NS-CQDs were used for spectrofluorometric determination of ASC in pharmaceutical dosage forms, with percentage recoveries ranging between 98 and 102 %, and relative standard deviations less than 2 %. The method was easy, rapid, reliable, and sensitive and did not require expensive reagents or sophisticated equipment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (ASC, vitamin C) is one of the most important water-soluble vitamins, naturally present in food, and commonly incorporated as food industry and in pharmaceuticals [1]. ASC has been widely used in the last years in COVID-19 therapeutic regimens [2] to enhance the immune function through stimulating lymphocyte and neutrophil activity. The accumulation of ASC intracellularly triggers the phagocytosis of the virus. Moreover, patients with COVID-19 infection may require more ASC due to serious oxidative stress [3]. Therefore, various analytical methods have been reported for quantitative determination of ASC in pharmaceutical formulations [4], fruit juices [5] and biological fluids [6]. These methods include HPLC [7,8], UV spectrophotometry [9,10], flow injection analysis [11], turbidimetry [12], potentiometry [13] electrochemical sensors [14] and spectrofluorometry [15,16]. Compared with the other techniques for ASC determination, spectrofluorometry is less sophisticated than HPLC, more reliable than turbidimetry, and potentiometry and more sensitive than UV spectrophotometry. However, spectrofluorometry can only be used to directly measure drugs with native fluorescence. Efforts have been exerted to chemically derivatize ASC with fluorogenic agents to enable spectrofluorometric detection, but the derivatization reactions were laborious, time and energy consuming [17,18]. For these reasons, there is an urgent need to find an accurate, reliable, rapid, and easy method for ASC determination.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a recent class of quantum dots, have attracted close attention due to the ease of synthesis, the cheap starting materials, the availability of carbon sources, the simplicity of surface modification, the water solubility, the low toxicity and the chemical stability [19–23]. CQDs have been applied successfully in different disciplines such as chemical sensing [24–26], photocatalysis [27], and bioimaging [28]. Several attempts were developed to enhance the physical and chemical properties of CQDs and to overcome the limitation of the low quantum yield [29]. Functionalization with surface passivation and heteroatom doping are efficient ways to improve the properties of CQDs [30] and to increase the quantum yield. Doping with nitrogen and sulfur is the most widely used, because these atoms decrease the energy gap [31,32]. CQDs were synthesized by different techniques, among which the microwave method was considered easy, eco-friendly, cost-effective, with high yields, and uniform particle size distribution [33].

This study aims to develop a green sensitive spectrofluorometric method for determination of ASC. The method depends on the simple preparation of NS-CQDs using citric acid as a carbon source and l-cysteine as sulfur and nitrogen source. The developed NS-CQDs have been successfully applied as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of ASC in dosage forms by restoring the native fluorescence of CQDs after initial quenching with ferric ions.

ASC 99.00 %, citric acid 99.00 %, l-cysteine 98.9 % were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Ferric chloride 99.2 %, copper sulfate 99.5 %, sodium chloride 99.5 %, chromium sulfate 98.8 %, cobaltous acetate 99.2 %, silver nitrate 98.9 %, magnesium sulfate 99.00 %, potassium iodide 98.7 %, and calcium chloride 99.3 % were purchased from Alpha Chemia Lab reagent (Mumbai, India). All the chemicals used in this experiment were of analytical grade. Distilled water was utilized throughout the experiments. A dialyzing membrane of 0.45 µm pore size was utilized for preparation NS-CQDs. C-VIT (100 μg/mL, Universal Pharmaceutical Industries,UNIPHARMA) oral drops was purchased from a local pharmacy.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using JASCO FP-6300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with slit widths (5 nm) at a scanning speed of 1000 nm.min−1 equipped with Spectra Manager software (V1.53.01). Shimadzu UV-1800 double beam spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) with a UV Probe 2.33 software was utilized for UV spectra processing. Tornado Microwave oven (1000 W) model MOM-C36BBE-S was used for the preparation of CQDs. The pH measurements were performed using HANNA pH-meter with a double junction glass electrode calibrated by standard buffers.

For the preparation of NS-CQDs, 0.4 g l-cysteine and 0.2 g citric acid were mixed in 10 mL beaker. Then, 4 mL of distilled water was added, followed by ultrasonication. After that, the mixture was placed in a Pyrex plate and heated in a microwave oven (800 W) for 60 sec till the formation of highly fluorescent NS-CQDs. A thick yellow syrup was obtained, diluted with distilled water, and dialyzed through a 0.45 µm membrane. The dialysate was left to cool and was stored at room temperature until use. Fluorescence intensities were measured at 440 nm after excitation at 350 nm.

The fluorescence intensities and the absorbance were measured for both ASC and quinine sulfate (QS) after excitation at 350 nm and the quantum yield (Y) of NS-CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard [34].
Y C Q D = Y Q S
F
CQD
F
QS
A
CQD
A
QS
η
CQD
η
QS
2
where F refers to the fluorescence, A refers to the absorbance and
η
refers to the refractive index of the solvent. The quantum yield of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (YQS) is 0.54 and ηCQD = ηQS equals 1.33 because both CQDs and QS were measured in the same solvent. To normalize the absorbance effect, the absorbance value was set at 0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stock and working solutions for calibration
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of ASC were prepared by dissolving 0.01 g in 10 mL distilled water. Different aliquots (50–1000 μL) of ASC were added to 300 μL of ferric solution and 130 μL NS-CQDs in 10 mL flask, then the volume was completed to the mark with the diluent to obtain final ASC concentrations in the range of 5.0–100 μg/mL. Fluorescence intensities were measured at 440 nm after excitation at 350 nm against the blank. The blank was prepared in 10 mL volumetric flask by the addition of 130 μL NS-CQDs and 300 μL ferric solution and adjusting to the volume with distilled water. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the concentration of ASC against the fluorescence intensity.

A volume of 100 µL of C-VIT oral drops was diluted with distilled water in 10 mL volumetric flask to obtain a final concentration of 1.0 mg/mL. Then, an aliquot of 200 µL of this solution was further diluted with distilled water in 10 mL flask to obtain a final concentration of 20 μg/mL. The resulting solution was sonicated and filtered with 0.45 μm membrane using a syringe filter before measurement. The same procedures in section 2.6 were followed and the emission was recorded at 440 nm using the spectrofluorometer and the % recoveries were calculated. The results were compared with the % recoveries obtained using the reported HPLC method. Fig. 1 summarizes the procedures of the spectrofluorometric determination of ascorbic acid using NS-CQDs.

Optimization of the experimental conditions during CQDs preparation is crucial to guarantee high quantum yield. The different variables that could affect the resultant CQDs quantum yield were studied, including the reaction time and the excitation wavelength. Different reaction times were tried and it was found that the optimum reaction time was 60 s. Increasing the reaction time above 60 s induced charring, while 50 s or less were not sufficient for CQD formation. To study the effect of the excitation wavelength, the emission spectra of NS-CQDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 380 nm. The emission intensity reached a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm with a maximum emission at 440 nm, as shown in Figure S1 . This phenomenon could be explained by the different surface functional groups on the NS-CQDs [35]. This will be further investigated in the next section.

Characterization of the NS-CQDs is essential to examine the morphology, particle size, distribution and surface functionality. The images of transmission electron microscopy(TEM) were captured to study the morphology and the size distribution of prepared NS-CQDs. Fig. 2 shows that the proposed NS-CQDs were homogenous, spherical shape and monodispersed. Additionally, FTIR of the obtained NS-CQDs was performed for chemical characterization (Fig. 3 ). The wavenumber from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 is characteristic of the –OH group. The spectrum shows a band at 3481 and 3451 cm−1, which could be due to the stretching bands of the –NH2 group. The band at 2371 cm−1 could be due to the -SH group, while the band at 645 cm−1 could be induced by the –NH bending vibrations. The quantum yield of the prepared NS-CQDs was 62.5 % compared to the quinine sulfate. This high quantum yield was caused by doping with nitrogen and sulfur atoms. Elemental analysis was performed for the further characterization of CQDs, the results revealed three main peaks for C,N,S with percentage compositions of 30.69 %, 11.29 % and 12.43 %, respectively illustrating the presence of O-rich groups on the surface of carbon frame. The abundant oxygen-containing groups endow the C-dots with good water solubility. The particle size distribution histogram is shown in Figure S2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the quenching metal cation
ce:section.ce:para: Proper selection of the optimum metal ion that turns off the fluorescence of CQDs is a crucial step. Different metal ions were studied, including alkali, alkaline earth metals and transition metals. The results showed that Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cr3+ had no or slight effects on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, while Ag+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+ induced partial quenching. This could be due to the vacant d orbitals in the transition metals, which facilitate non-radiative deactivation of the excited state to the ground state. As shown in Figure S3 , the maximum quenching was observed with Fe3+, so it was selected for turning off the emission of CQDs before it could be restored by the addition of ASC, as shown in Fig. 4 .

The effects of pH, incubation time and ionic strength on the emission intensity were studied. It was found that the fluorescence intensities decreased when the pH decreased below 1.5 or increased above 2.5 as shown in Figure S4 . Therefore, pH 2.0 was selected as a diluent to give the maximum and the most reproducible fluorescence intensity. The incubation time was studied after the addition of ASC to NS-CQDs. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at different time intervals from 1 min to 25 min. The reaction between ASC and NS-CQDs was found to be rapid and complete within 1.0 min and the fluorescence signals were stable for 25 min. The ionic strength was investigated by adding different concentrations of potassium iodide, KI (1 μg/mL) to N, S-CQS solutions. The results show that the ionic strength had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQS. This could be explained by the lack of ionization groups on the surface of the N,S-CQS.

The proposed method was validated according to the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) Q2 guidelines, including linearity, range, accuracy, precision, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), specificity and robustness. The results were compared with the acceptance limits to ensure that the developed method was valid and reliable.

The fluorescence intensity was directly proportional to the concentration of ASC, as shown in Fig. 5 . The calibration curve was linear over the concentration range 2.0–100 μg/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.9965. The results of regression parameters are summarized in Table 1 .

Both limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated according to the ICH guideline Q2 (R1), based on the standard deviation of blank samples (σ) and the slope of the calibration curve (S) by using the following equation.
LOD = 3.3 σ/S LOQ = 10 σ/S.
The calculated LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.6 and 2 μg/mL, respectively, which indicates that the method has sufficient sensitivity for the determination of ASC in pharmaceutical dosage forms.

The accuracy was measured by three replicates of three different concentrations within the linearity range. It was found that the calculated mean % recoveries were within the range (98–102 %) which confirm the accuracy of the method. The precision was measured by three replicates of three different concentrations within the linearity range in the same day (intra-day) and over three successive days (inter-day). The results, summarized in Table 2
, showed that the %RSD was less than 2 %, which indicate that the method was sufficiently precise.

The Robustness of the proposed method was studied through inducing minor changes in the experimental factors that could affect the fluorescence intensity, including the emission wavelength, ƛex (350 nm ± 2), the excitation wavelength, ƛem (440 nm ± 2) and -the pH (2.0 ± 0.2). The results showed no significant effects of the studied variables on the percentage recovery as shown in Table S1 . This indicates that the method could withstand small changes in experimental conditions without affecting the resultant %recoveries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The greenness of the developed method was measured using the analytical eco-scale and the green analytical procedure index (GAPI) [36,37]. The analytical eco-scale is a quantitative tool, which depends on penalty points (pps) subtracted from a base of 100. Method with scores above 75 is considered excellent green, methods with scores between 50 and 75 is acceptable, while methods with scores below 50 is inadequately green. As shown in Table 3 , the proposed method was given a score of 90, which confirms that the method is excellent, compared with the reference method which had a total score of 64. This is because the HPLC method requires larger amounts of organic solvents, produces larger amounts of waste and consumes more energy.

The method greenness was also assessed by the GAPI, which consists of five pentagrams covering the entire analytical procedures, starting from sample preparation to final determination. Each section in the pentagram was given one of three colors (green, yellow and red) to indicate low, medium or high environmental effects, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , the developed method was greener than the reference method since the solvent used in this study was distilled water and the method did not involve any sample preparation steps, or occupational hazard, with less than 10 mL waste amount per sample.

The proposed method was applied for determination of ASC in C-VIT oral drops. The mean % recoveries and % RSD were calculated. The oral drops were also assayed using the reported HPLC method as shown in Figure S5 and the results were statistically compared [38] using Student’s t-test and F-test. The results showed no statistically significant differences between the proposed fluorescence method and the reference HPLC/UV method as shown in Table 4 .

Highly fluorescent NS-CQDs were prepared and utilized as a nanoprobe for accurate quantitative determination of ASC in pharmaceutical dosage forms without the need for chemical derivatization. Based on a fluorescence “off/on” mechanism, ferric ions had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of CQDs via the dynamic mechanism, then the addition of ASC restored the fluorescence of the CQDs. The method adopted for synthesis of NS-CQDs (microwave method) has been carefully optimized, regarding the reaction time and the excitation wavelength. In addition, all critical factors affecting the fluorescence intensity were investigated. Generally, the developed method was simple, green, and cost effective. Also, the method can be efficiently used for quality control studies of ASC in raw material and in pharmaceutical dosage forms.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122440.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
